text.fullText: From: Elizabeth Toohig <noreply@myfor.ms> To: OW-Docket <OW-Docket@epa.gov> Date: 2/2/2026 9:49:26 AM Subject: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 -------------------------------------------- You don't often get email from noreply@myfor.ms. Learn why this is important Caution: This email originated from outside EPA, please exercise additional caution when deciding whether to open attachments or click on provided links. I VEHEMENTLY oppose the EPA’s proposed revisions to the Clean Water Act Section 401 water quality certification regulations.(As I'm writing this, I'm wondering if the EPA stands for environmental PROTECTION!!) The proposal would unlawfully and dangerously narrow the authority of states and tribes to protect their waters from harmful projects — especially large oil and gas pipelines. By limiting review to narrowly defined “discharges” and constraining timelines and conditions, the rule undermines Congress’s clear intent that states and tribes serve as frontline protectors of water quality. This rollback is especially concerning for the Great Lakes, which hold 20% of the world’s surface fresh water and supply drinking water to more than 40 million people. In Michigan, the proposal could directly affect decisions regarding Enbridge’s Line 5 oil pipeline, including a proposed tunnel and potential horizontal directional drilling beneath the Straits of Mackinac—an area of extraordinary ecological, cultural, and economic significance. Enbridge has delayed a final decision on its Clean Water Act Section 404 permit from the Michigan Department of Environment, Great Lakes, and Energy. If EPA finalizes this rule, it could affect the outcome by restricting Michigan’s ability—and tribal nations’ treaty-protected authority—to fully evaluate and reject water quality risks. States and tribes MUST RETAIN THE POWER to: • Consider cumulative and long-term water quality impacts • Protect treaty rights, fisheries, and drinking water • Deny certification when projects threaten water quality standards The EPA should withdraw this proposal and instead strengthen, not weaken, Clean Water Act protections. The Great Lakes are too important to risk for the convenience of oil pipeline companies. EPA members: PLEASE CONSIDER EVEN JUST THE NAME OF YOUR ORGANIZATION AND RESIGN IF YOU CAN'T LIVE UP TO THAT. Think of what your grandchildren will think of you if they hear you approved provisions that would weaken the Clean Water Act. Sincerely, Elizabeth Toohig Wellesley Hills, MA
